subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The heart of the chapter is summarising and comparing real data using measures of central tendency — it develops the arithmetic mean as sum-over-count and as fair-share, the median of sorted data, the idea of an outlier, and how outliers shift the mean relative to the median, applying all of these to heights, scores, prices, and survey data.

Section 13.3 has students read and construct appropriate graphical representations — dot plots and clustered (double) bar graphs — with chosen scales, and use a two-step identify-and-infer process to interpret rocket-launch, daylight-hour, temperature, and vehicle-registration graphs, which is the selecting, creating, and using of graphs to make interpretations.

adjunct_competencies.justification: The 'Data Detective' section trains students to distinguish claims that the data justifies from those it does not — judging statements about height trends across 1989–2019, rocket launches, and pocket counts — and to justify claims about the mean and median, a sustained exercise in producing convincing, evidence-based arguments.

The chapter records the ancient Indian terminology for the arithmetic mean, attributing samarajju to Brahmagupta (628 CE), samīkaraṇa to Mahāvīrācārya (850 CE), sāmya to Śrīpati (1039 CE), and samamiti to Bhāskarācārya (1150 CE) and Gaṇeṣa (1545 CE), an explicit appreciation of Indian mathematicians' contributions.

Computing means and medians of the chapter's real datasets applies decimal arithmetic in daily-life situations throughout — averaging heights to figures like 164.3 cm and 160.2 cm, comparing water-usage values such as 5.6, 3.09, and 12.9 litres, and sumo-wrestler and ballet-dancer weights in kilograms.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to summarise and compare data using the mean, median, and range, and to construct and interpret dot plots and clustered bar graphs, by collecting real data and reasoning about what representative values and visualisations reveal.
